words.definition: Acting quickly on a sudden urge without stopping to weigh the consequences, often with a negative or reckless implication.

exercises.corrected: I really must object to being made to wait so long before seeing the manager.

words.definition: Intervene means to step into a situation to help or affect its outcome (neutral); interfere means to involve yourself in matters that are not your concern (negative, critical).
words.example: The referee had to intervene to separate the players, but the fans should not interfere in decisions that are not theirs to make.

words.definition: Continually means very frequently and repeatedly (often with breaks and often annoying); continuously means without any interruption or stopping.
words.example: The alarm went off continually all week, whereas the fountain runs continuously day and night.
words.translationHint: again and again vs without a break

words.definition: A series is a set of related but self-contained programmes or items; a serial tells one continuing story across successive episodes.
words.example: I can watch any episode of that documentary series, but the drama is a serial, so I must follow it from the start.

words.definition: Shade is an area sheltered from direct sunlight (pleasant); a shadow is the dark shape cast by an object blocking light.
words.example: We rested in the shade of the oak tree while the setting sun stretched our shadows across the grass.
words.translationHint: cool sheltered area vs dark cast shape

words.definition: To compliment someone is to praise them; to complement something is to combine well with it and make it more complete.
words.example: The critic complimented the chef, noting that the sharp sauce perfectly complemented the rich meat.

words.definition: Moist means slightly wet, often in a pleasant or desirable way; damp means slightly wet in an unpleasant, unwanted way.
words.example: A good sponge cake should be moist, but the old cellar felt cold and damp.

words.definition: A theme is the central underlying idea running through something; a topic is simply the subject being discussed at a given moment.
words.example: The theme of the novel is forgiveness, though the topic of the opening chapter is merely a village fair.

words.definition: Security concerns protection against deliberate threats such as theft or attack; safety concerns protection against accidents and harm.
words.example: The security team guards against intruders, while the safety officer makes sure everyone wears a helmet.
words.translationHint: protection from threats vs from accidents

words.definition: To avoid something is to stay away from it; to evade something is to escape or dodge it, often cleverly and more formally.
words.example: We took the back road to avoid the traffic, but the suspect managed to evade the police for weeks.

words.definition: A breakout is an escape, especially from prison; an outbreak is the sudden start of something bad such as a disease or war.
words.example: There was a breakout at the jail on the same night an outbreak of flu closed the local school.
words.translationHint: escape vs sudden start of something bad

words.definition: The outlook is the likely future prospect for a situation; a lookout is a person who keeps watch, or the act of watching for something.
words.example: The economic outlook is gloomy, so the firm posted a lookout for any early signs of falling sales.
words.translationHint: future prospect vs person keeping watch

words.definition: Set up (verb) means to organise or establish something; setup (noun) is the way something is arranged or organised.
words.example: They set up a new branch abroad, and the whole company setup is now far more complex.

words.definition: To upend something is to turn it upside down or into a vertical position; to end up is to finally reach a state or place.
words.example: He upended the box to empty it, and somehow we ended up spending the whole afternoon sorting the contents.
words.translationHint: turn upside down vs finally become/reach

words.definition: To uphold something is to confirm or support it; to hold up means to delay something, or to rob it.
words.example: The court chose to uphold the original verdict, though the paperwork continued to hold up his release.

words.definition: To outdo someone is to do better than they do; to do out (a room) is to decorate or clean it thoroughly.
words.example: The neighbours always try to outdo each other, so they did out their entire house before the party.

words.definition: To outrun someone is to run faster than them; to run out means to use up all of something so none is left.
words.example: The fox easily outran the hounds, but the hunters ran out of daylight before they could regroup.
words.translationHint: run faster than vs use up completely

words.definition: To upset someone is to make them worried, unhappy or angry; to set up means to organise or arrange something.
words.example: It upset her to learn that colleagues had set up the meeting without telling her.

words.definition: Affect is usually a verb meaning to influence or make a difference to; effect is usually a noun meaning the result of a change.
words.example: The new policy will affect thousands of workers, and its full effect may not be clear for years.

words.definition: Principal means main or most important, or the head of a school; a principle is a fundamental rule or belief.
words.example: The principal reason she refused the deal was a matter of principle, not money.

words.definition: Stationary means not moving; stationery refers to writing materials such as paper, pens and envelopes.
words.example: The traffic was stationary for an hour, so I sorted the office stationery while I waited.

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct word: 'Stir the sauce ___ until it thickens' (from a recipe, meaning without stopping).
Which sentence uses the words correctly?
options: She complemented me on my speech.
The wine complemented the cheese, and she complimented the chef.

exercises.prompt: 'The medicine will ___ his heart rate, and the ___ should last several hours.' Which pair fits?
The UN decided to ___ in the conflict, but neighbouring states were warned not to interfere.
The escaped convict managed to ___ capture for three months by hiding in the mountains.
The cars sat completely ___ in the jam, so I bought some ___ at the shop to write my letters.

pairs.definition: sudden start of something bad, e.g. disease
an escape, especially from prison
to confirm or support
to delay or to rob
the likely future prospect

exercises.prompt: The cellar was so moist and cold that the walls had turned green with mould.
exercises.corrected: The cellar was so damp and cold that the walls had turned green with mould.

words.definition: The genuine version of something, as opposed to a substitute, copy or imitation.
words.example: Video calls are useful, but nothing beats meeting your family in person for the real thing.
words.translationHint: the genuine version, not a substitute

words.definition: Something that is authentic and real rather than a fake or cheap imitation.
words.example: These boots are the genuine article; the ones at the market stall are just cheap copies.
words.translationHint: the authentic item, not a fake

words.definition: A general overview that covers the main points without going into detail. Note we say 'broad', not 'wide'.
words.example: Could you give us a broad summary of the report before we look at the figures in detail?

words.definition: With a lot of specific information and thoroughness. Note we say 'great detail', not 'big detail'.
words.example: The witness described the incident in great detail, mentioning even the colour of the car.

words.definition: A rival who is impressively strong, capable and difficult to defeat.
words.example: In the final she faced a formidable opponent who had not lost a match all season.

words.definition: A standing that inspires respect, and often some fear, because of impressive ability or achievement.
words.example: The professor has a formidable reputation for exposing weak arguments in seminars.

words.definition: A job or duty that is very demanding and will require great effort to complete.
words.example: Rebuilding the bridge before winter is a formidable task for such a small team.

words.definition: A test or difficulty that is daunting and will be hard to overcome.
words.example: Learning three languages in a year is a formidable challenge, but she is determined to try.

words.definition: To move on foot at a fast pace. 'Quickly' is the natural adverb with 'walk', unlike 'walk fast'.
words.example: We had to walk quickly to reach the platform before the doors closed.

words.definition: To proceed rapidly, especially to advance to the next point or stage without delay.
words.example: Let's move swiftly on to the budget, as we're running short of time.
words.translationHint: proceed rapidly to the next point

words.definition: To have powerful opinions or emotions concerning a particular issue. Note 'strongly', not 'powerfully'.
words.example: She feels strongly about recycling and refuses to throw away anything reusable.

words.definition: A phrase used to introduce something you believe to be true from memory. Note 'rightly', not 'perfectly'.
words.example: If I remember rightly, the shop closes early on Sundays, so we should go now.
words.translationHint: as far as I recall correctly

words.definition: To lift your hand into the air, typically to ask or answer a question. Note 'raise', not 'lift'.
words.example: Please raise your hand if you have a question rather than calling out.

words.definition: To bring up children and care for them as they grow. Note 'raise', not 'lift'.
words.example: They moved to the countryside because they wanted to raise a family away from the city.

words.definition: To go to and view a website. 'Visit', 'go to', 'click on' and 'check out' all collocate with 'website'.
words.example: Visit our website to see the full range of courses on offer.

words.definition: To press a mouse button, or tap, on a link or item on a screen to open or select it.
words.example: Just click on the link in the email to confirm your booking.

words.definition: To look at a website to see what it offers; informal.
words.example: You should check out their website; they've just added a lot of free tutorials.
words.translationHint: have a look at a website (informal)

words.definition: Completely shocking and dreadful. 'Utterly' frequently modifies strongly negative adjectives.
words.example: The conditions in the old factory were utterly appalling by any modern standard.

words.definition: Completely absurd and unreasonable.
words.example: The idea that one person could finish all this work in a day is utterly ridiculous.

words.definition: Completely lacking any purpose or use.
words.example: Arguing with him once he's made up his mind is utterly pointless.

exercises.prompt: Which collocation is standard English?
Complete naturally: 'The report describes the process in ___.'
Which adjective collocates naturally with 'opponent', 'reputation', 'task' and 'challenge'?
It's an issue I ___ strongly about, so I always vote in local elections.
Please ___ your hand if you'd like to volunteer for the project.
The service was so bad it was ___ appalling; we'll never go back.

pairs.definition: the authentic item, not a fake
proceed rapidly to the next point
bring up children
as far as I recall correctly
completely purposeless or useless

exercises.prompt: They bought a house in the suburbs because they wanted to lift a family there.
exercises.corrected: They bought a house in the suburbs because they wanted to raise a family there.

words.definition: A city or built-up area seen metaphorically as wild, chaotic and full of dangers, like a jungle.
words.example: After years in a quiet village, she found the noise and rush of the urban jungle overwhelming.

words.definition: To make a person's life brighter and happier, as a light brightens a dark place.
words.example: Their new granddaughter has completely lit up their lives since she was born.

words.definition: To be alert, quick to understand and ready to act, like an attentive footballer.
words.example: The new assistant is really on the ball; she spotted the error before anyone else did.
words.translationHint: be alert and quick to react

words.word: keep someone on a tight rein
words.definition: To control someone or something strictly, like a rider tightly controlling a horse.
words.example: The manager keeps the interns on a tight rein until they've learned the procedures.

words.definition: The central part of a city, using 'heart' metaphorically to mean the middle or core.
words.example: The cathedral stands right in the heart of the city, surrounded by narrow old streets.
words.translationHint: the very centre of the city

words.definition: The place where a river flows into the sea, using 'mouth' metaphorically for an opening.
words.example: Fishing boats gather near the mouth of the river where it widens into the bay.
words.translationHint: where a river meets the sea

words.definition: The person in charge of a company or group, using 'head' metaphorically for the leader.
words.example: As the head of the organisation, she takes final responsibility for every major decision.

words.definition: To have a natural talent for noticing or judging a particular kind of thing well.
words.example: He has a real eye for a bargain and always finds the best deals at the market.

words.definition: To watch someone or something carefully and attentively.
words.example: Please keep a close eye on the soup so it doesn't boil over while I'm out.

words.definition: Being looked after by someone capable and trustworthy.
words.example: Don't worry about the accounts; with Maria in charge, they're in safe hands.
words.translationHint: being cared for by someone capable

words.definition: To exist together and be closely connected, as if holding hands.
words.example: In this job, patience goes hand in hand with attention to detail.

words.definition: A rough, practical method of estimating that is not exact but usually accurate enough.
words.example: As a rule of thumb, allow about two hours of study for every hour in class.

words.definition: To finally understand or realise something, drawing on the metaphor of light as understanding.
words.example: After weeks of confusion, he finally saw the light and grasped how the system worked.

words.definition: To make something easier to understand or explain, as light reveals what is in darkness.
words.example: The newly found diaries cast light on the writer's mysterious final years.

words.definition: To keep changing between enthusiasm and lack of interest, using temperature as a metaphor for feeling.
words.example: He's been blowing hot and cold about the job offer, so we still don't know his answer.

words.definition: Quick to become angry and to act on emotion without thinking first.
words.example: Try not to be so hotheaded; count to ten before you reply to that email.

words.definition: Lacking warmth, sympathy or feeling towards others.
words.example: It was cold-hearted of them to evict the family just before the holidays.

words.definition: A continuous flow of vehicles, comparing movement of traffic to flowing water.
words.example: A constant stream of traffic passed the window all through the night.

words.definition: To start an organised series of actions aimed at a goal, using military language for business or social action.
words.example: The charity will launch a campaign next month to raise awareness of clean water.

words.definition: To bring worthwhile advantages later, comparing effort or time to a financial investment.
words.example: The hours she spent practising every day are now paying dividends in her performances.

exercises.prompt: If someone 'blows hot and cold' about a plan, they are...
options: keep changing their level of enthusiasm

exercises.prompt: Which phrase means to make something easier to understand?
Saying your savings are 'in safe hands' means they are...
options: being looked after by someone capable

the person in charge
be closely connected with
quick-tempered and impulsive
finally understand something

exercises.prompt: The old market building stands right in the middle heart of city, surrounded by cafes.
exercises.corrected: The old market building stands right in the heart of the city, surrounded by cafes.

words.definition: To succeed absolutely perfectly, with no problems at all.
words.example: We were nervous about the new booking system, but on the first day it worked like a dream.

words.definition: To happen smoothly and exactly as planned, without any difficulties.
words.example: Thanks to careful rehearsal, the whole ceremony went like clockwork.

words.definition: To have started working or operating as intended.
words.example: It took a week to install everything, but the new office is now up and running.

words.definition: For the parts of a situation to come together and start working out well.
words.example: Once we found a reliable supplier, everything else quickly fell into place.
words.translationHint: come together and work out well

words.definition: For a situation to be improving and becoming more positive.
words.example: After a difficult year, business is finally looking up again.

words.definition: A sarcastic response to news that makes an already difficult situation even worse.
words.example: Rain, and now a flat tyre too? That's all we need!

words.word: that's the last thing I wanted to hear
words.definition: A response used when news confirms your worst fears.
words.example: The flight's cancelled? That's the last thing I wanted to hear before an important meeting.
words.translationHint: exactly the bad news I dreaded

words.definition: Used to describe a situation in which one bad thing happens after another in quick succession.
words.example: First the taxi didn't come, then I lost my wallet; the whole morning was like a bad dream.

words.definition: A general expression for any extremely unpleasant or stressful experience.
words.example: Parking in the city centre on a Saturday is a real nightmare.

words.definition: A general expression of annoyance in response to something that causes difficulty or inconvenience.
words.example: The lift's broken again and I live on the tenth floor. What a pain!

words.definition: To confuse or unsettle someone so much that they don't know how to react.
words.example: Her unexpected question threw me completely, and I forgot what I'd planned to say.
words.translationHint: confuse someone so they can't respond

words.definition: A totally disorganised, chaotic and badly run situation or event.
words.example: With no schedule and no one in charge, the conference was a complete shambles.

words.definition: To misunderstand each other because of a miscommunication.
words.example: We must have got our wires crossed; I thought you were picking me up, not the other way round.

words.definition: A phrase used to say that you don't understand what someone has just said.
words.example: Sorry, I'm not with you; could you explain that last step again?

words.definition: A phrase used to say that an explanation has confused you and you can no longer follow it.
words.example: You've lost me there; what does the second figure actually refer to?

words.word: be on top of the world
words.definition: To feel extremely happy and pleased.
words.example: Ever since she passed her final exams, she's been on top of the world.

words.definition: To be extremely happy and delighted.
words.example: He's been on cloud nine since they told him he got the job.

words.definition: To be delighted and extremely pleased about something.
words.example: They were over the moon when they heard the baby had arrived safely.

words.definition: To be doing something you enjoy and are naturally good at, and feeling completely comfortable.
words.example: Give him a garden and some tools and he's in his element all weekend.
words.translationHint: be in a situation ideally suited to you

words.definition: To feel sad, low or depressed.
words.example: She's been down in the dumps since her best friend moved abroad.
